text: This paper investigates the extent to which the administrative judiciary in Jordan achieves judicial control over administrative actions in the era of its transition from a single-tier judiciary—the old Supreme Court of Justice—to a two-tier judiciary—comprising the Administrative Court and the Supreme Administrative Court. This transition enhanced the degree of confidence in justice due to the fact that administrative cases are subject to review by two levels of judiciary, thereby bringing Jordanian law closer to the minimum international standards for the protection of the rule of law and the right to a fair trial. Using an applied empirical approach, the study relies on the statistical data provided by the Administrative Court for the year 2020 and shows a clear increase in the number of judicial cases that were filed before the administrative judiciary in 2020 compared to 2019. The study reveals a number of challenges and shortcomings faced by the administrative judiciary in Jordan, mainly the lack of a sufficient number of administrative courts in the provinces, as first-instance administrative courts are only located in Amman, while the Supreme Administrative Court is also located in Amman. This calls for the need to achieve judicial justice by establishing administrative courts of first instance and appeal in each of the country’s provinces. In addition, the study calls for the necessity of clarifying the principle of the competence of administrative judiciary so as to determine the scope of jurisdiction exercised by the ordinary judiciary on the one hand, and to clarify the competent judicial authority in administrative disputes on the other hand.
